content.problemStatement:
You recently read about the Caesar Cipher, one of the first and the easiest to break cryptographic algorithms known.

Since it was very easy to break, you decided to improve it by building a lite version of it. Here is your modified algorithm.

Suppose you want to encrypt a plaintext PPP of length NNN.

First, you generate a secret cryptographic key KKK which is an array of length NNN. Then, for each iii from 111 to NNN, you perform KiK_iKi​ Caesar 333-shifts on character PiP_iPi​.

A Caesar 333-shift is defined as replacing a character with the 3rd3^{rd}3rd next character in the the English alphabet. This is done cyclically, meaning the character `'A'` is the next character to the character `'Z'`. For example, performing a single 333-shift on `'Y'` will result in `'B'`.

Let's denote the encrypted ciphertext of a plaintext PPP using a key KKK as EK(P)E_K(P)EK​(P).

For example, if K=[40,0,14,15]K=[40,0,14,15]K=[40,0,14,15], EK("PASS")="FAIL"E_K(\texttt{"PASS"})=\texttt{"FAIL"}EK​("PASS")="FAIL".

Now after a long time, you forgot what KKK was. However, you have a plaintext PPP and its encrypted ciphertext CCC. You task is to find the lexicographically smallest array KKK such that EK(P)=CE_K(P)=CEK​(P)=C.

It can be shown that such a KKK always exists.

An array XXX is lexicographically smaller than another array YYY of the same length if in the first position iii where XXX and YYY differ, Xi<YiX_i\lt Y_iXi​<Yi​.


Input Format
The first line of input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of testcases.


Each testcase contains three lines of input:



The first line contains a single integer NNN, the length of the strings.
The second line contains a single strings PPP, the plaintext.
The third line contains a single strings CCC, the encrypted ciphertext.


Output Format
For each testcase, print NNN space-separated integers on a single line, representing the lexicographically smallest key KKK such that EK(P)=CE_K(P)=CEK​(P)=C.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^5
P and C consist only of uppercase letters 'A'‑'Z'
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the strings. The second line contains a string P of length N (the plaintext). The third line contains a string C of length N (the ciphertext). All characters are uppercase English letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with N space‑separated integers K1 K2 … KN – the lexicographically smallest key that transforms P into C using the described 3‑shift Caesar cipher.

sampleTestCases.input:
3
3
ABC
GBY
4
PASS
FAIL
5
SEVEN
EIGHT
sampleTestCases.explanation: For each position we compute diff = (C_i - P_i) mod 26 and then Ki = (diff·9) mod 26. The resulting arrays are the lexicographically smallest keys that satisfy the encryption.

solution.approach:
For each position i we need Ki such that applying Ki times a 3‑shift Caesar cipher to Pi yields Ci. A single 3‑shift moves a character forward by 3 positions in the alphabet, therefore Ki 3‑shifts move it forward by 3·Ki positions. Working modulo 26 we need:
    (Pi + 3·Ki) mod 26 = Ci
=> 3·Ki ≡ diff (mod 26) where diff = (Ci - Pi + 26) mod 26.
Since gcd(3,26)=1, 3 has a modular inverse modulo 26. The inverse of 3 is 9 because 3·9 = 27 ≡ 1 (mod 26). Multiplying both sides by 9 gives the unique solution in the range [0,25]:
    Ki = (diff · 9) mod 26.
Any other solution would be Ki + 26·t (t ≥ 1) and would be larger, so the above value is the smallest possible for this position. Positions are independent, therefore the lexicographically smallest whole array is obtained by taking the minimal Ki for every i.
The algorithm simply iterates over the characters, computes diff, applies the formula, and stores the result.
Time complexity is O(N) per test case, total O(∑N). Memory usage is O(1) besides the input strings.
solution.timeComplexity: O(∑ N) – linear in the total length of all strings.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        P = next(it).strip()
        C = next(it).strip()
        ks = []
        for p_char, c_char in zip(P, C):
            p = ord(p_char) - 65
            c = ord(c_char) - 65
            diff = (c - p) % 26
            k = (diff * 9) % 26  # 9 is modular inverse of 3 mod 26
            ks.append(str(k))
        out_lines.append(' '.join(ks))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
